title: Irish potato bread
subtitle: Pan-fried flat bread of mashed potato and flour
excerpt: Irish potato bread is a soft, flat griddle bread made from mashed potato and a small amount of flour, pan-fried until golden. It is a staple of the Ulster fry and serves equally well as a side for eggs or eaten on its own with butter. Ready in under 20 minutes from start to plate.
sourceNotes: Potato bread (potato farls, tattie bread) is a traditional bread of Ireland and Northern Ireland. The basic formula of mashed potato, flour, and a little fat appears in nineteenth-century Irish domestic cookery texts. Florence Irwin, The Cookin' Woman (1949), documents the potato farl as a standard of Ulster kitchen baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked farls between sheets of baking paper. Reheat from frozen in a dry pan.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily; use a second pan simultaneously.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Refrigerate cooked farls and reheat in a dry pan to order.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: griddle or heavy pan, medium heat

content.text: Potato bread is a flat, pan-fried bread made from mashed potato and plain flour, no yeast, no waiting. The potato gives the farl a soft, slightly dense texture and a golden crust that picks up quickly in a hot pan. A standard Ulster fry plate has two alongside the soda bread; on its own, it is good with fried eggs or just spread with butter while it is still warm.

Mash the cooked potatoes thoroughly while still hot, working out all the lumps. Add the melted butter and salt, then sift in the flour and bring together into a soft dough. Knead lightly in the bowl for 30 seconds; just enough to incorporate the flour. The dough should be pliable and not sticky; if it sticks, add flour a tablespoon at a time.
Divide the dough in half. On a lightly floured surface, roll each half into a round, about 5 mm thick and 22 cm across. Cut each round into four quarters. You now have eight farls.

Heat a dry griddle or heavy frying pan over medium heat. Cook the farls in batches for 4 to 5 minutes per side. They are done when both sides are an even, pale gold and the farl feels firm when you press the centre lightly. Serve immediately or keep warm in a low oven.
